The SAZS framework is designed to address the task of zero-shot semantic segmentation by enforcing vision-language alignment, exploiting language priors, and aligning the boundary of predicted semantic regions with ground truth regions.
The SAZS framework utilizes the large-scale pre-trained vision-language model CLIP to enforce vision-language alignment during training.

node_2: eigenvectors of laplacian of affinity matrices
edge: The SAZS framework leverages the eigenvectors of Laplacian of affinity matrices to decompose inputs into eigensegments, which are then fused with learning-based predictions from the trained model.
The predictions of the approach are better aligned with the shapes of objects compared to previous methods, as illustrated in Figure 1.

The SAZS framework demonstrates the effectiveness and generalization by evaluating the correlation between the shape compactness of the target object and performance, highlighting the impact of object shapes on performance.
The SAZS framework analyzes the correlation between language embedding locality and performance, emphasizing the impact of language anchors and object shapes on performance.
The SAZS framework demonstrates effectiveness and generalization of shape perception for segmenting semantic categories in the open world.
Zero-Shot Semantic Segmentation task aims to perform pixel-wise predictions for objects that are unseen during training.
Recent works on Zero-Shot Semantic Segmentation have seen two main branches - generative methods and discriminative methods.
ZS3Net utilizes a generative model to create a visual representation of objects not present in the training data by leveraging pre-trained word embeddings.
CaGNet highlights the impact of contextual information on pixel-level features through a network learning to generate specific contextual pixel-level features.
CSRI introduces constraints to the generation of unseen visual features by exploiting structural relationships between seen and unseen categories.
SPNet leverages similarities between known categories to transfer learned representations to other unknown categories.
Baek et al. employ visual and semantic encoders to learn a joint embedding space with the semantic encoder converting semantic features into semantic prototypes.
Naoki et al. focus on learning representations for unknown categories by employing visual and semantic encoders.
The semantic encoder converts the semantic features into semantic prototypes.
Variational mapping projects the class embeddings from the semantic to the visual space.
Transductive approach using target images to mitigate prediction bias towards seen categories.
LSeg proposes a language-driven ZSS model, mapping pixels and names of labels into a shared embedding space for pixel-wise dense prediction.

Contrastive learning is developed in CLIP to train robust models for zero-shot image classification, but the direct utilization of large-scale pre-trained vision encoders for pixel-level classification work is unsatisfactory.
The direct utilization of image-level vision-language features does not consider the discrepancy between image-level and pixel-level dense prediction tasks.
Supervision plays a role in extending the semantic segmentation task to unseen categories in zero-shot semantic segmentation.
Semantic segmentation task involves segmenting objects in an image into different categories. The task also involves dealing with unseen categories, which are not present in the training datasets.
Most vision-language models focus on image-level prediction rather than dense prediction tasks like semantic segmentation.
Semantic segmentation task is a type of dense prediction task, which involves predicting pixel-wise labels for objects in an image.

CLIP model provides rich language priors for vision-language models, which can be leveraged for tasks like semantic segmentation.
Pre-trained CLIP model provides text embeddings that can be aligned with pixel-wise embeddings to improve pixel-wise prediction in tasks like semantic segmentation.
The image encoder transforms input images into pixel-wise embeddings, which are then aligned with text embeddings obtained from the pre-trained CLIP model for prediction tasks.
An extra head in the image encoder predicts boundaries in patches, which are then optimized towards ground-truth edges for segmentation tasks.

The concept of self-supervised spectral decomposition involves decomposing input images into eigensegments in an unsupervised manner.
Eigensegments obtained in self-supervised spectral decomposition are fused with the predictions of neural networks.
In the process of self-supervised spectral decomposition, eigensegments are combined with the affinity matrix derived from input images.
Features f from the attention block of the last layer of a pre-trained self-supervised transformer are leveraged in the derivation of the affinity matrix.
The affinities derived from transformer features contain rich semantic information.

The affinity between pixels is defined using the formula Zshape(i, j) = 1 − ∥X(i) − X(j)∥2, j ∈ KNN(i)(5), where ∥ · ∥2 denotes 2-norm.
The overall affinity matrix Z(i, j) is defined as the weighted sum of Zsem and λ · Zshape(6).
The eigenvectors of the Laplacian L of the affinity matrix are used to decompose the image into multiple eigensegments.
Phrases of the categories from the image are encoded using the pre-trained text encoder CLIP to obtain textual features.

Semantic features extracted using DINO are used to calculate the top K spectral eigensegments Ek.

We also demonstrate the importance of fusing with spectral eigensegments during inference. Without the fusion module, the mIoU dramatically decreases by 7.0% on PASCAL-5i and by 6.2% (ViT backbone) and 8.6% (DRN backbone) on COCO-20i.

We conduct an ablation experiment on the PASCAL-5i dataset to investigate the effects of Zsem and Zshape in our fusion module. As shown in Table 6, both Zsem and Zshape contribute to improved segmentation performance, but using Zsem alone yields better results than using Zshape alone.
